mnemonics: S.C.A.N. the data for proximity: Similarity (Cosine), Correlation, Absolute (Manhattan), Normalized (Euclidean).
Remember the 'M' in Manhattan: Moving along city blocks (grid) requires adding all the differences (Absolute values).
Cosine measures the angle, not the magnitude. Think of the 'direction' of the data, not its length.
keyPoints: Proximity Measures quantify how 'close' or 'similar' two data points are.
Euclidean Distance is the straight-line distance between points in a space (the most common measure).
Manhattan Distance is the sum of the absolute differences along each dimension, like moving on a city grid.
Cosine Similarity measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors, indicating orientation similarity regardless of magnitude.
commonMistakes: Using Euclidean distance on data with different scales without normalizing it first.
Treating Cosine Similarity as a distance metric (it's a similarity measure; 1 means identical, 0 means orthogonal).
Assuming a small Euclidean distance always implies high similarity, which may not hold in high-dimensional spaces (the 'curse of dimensionality').
